checks.a_analysis: The output JSON faithfully reproduces all paragraphs and headings from the source document. There is no evidence of fabricated content or hallucinated text. The signature area is represented as a table, which is a structural conversion choice, but the text content matches the source exactly.
The source document does not contain any phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs. The output also contains no contact information, thus no fabrication occurred.

The source document is a single-page resolution. Every component, including the location headers, logo placeholder, resolution title, 'WHEREAS' clauses, the final resolution text, the adoption date, and the signers' names and titles, are present in the output.

The source document does not contain any tables. The output represents the signature block as a table for structure, which is an acceptable reformatting choice. Since there is no source table to compare row counts against, this check passes.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All sections in the output are complete. Each 'WHEREAS' clause and the final resolving paragraph contain the full text as found in the source document without truncation.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output matches the source document with high fidelity. Notably, it preserves an inconsistency in the source document where one clause mentions 'Protective Services Occupations' while the final resolution mentions 'Protective Service Occupations'. The OCR accurately captured all names, dates, and technical terminology.

checks.a_analysis: The text content from the source PDF appears only once in the JSON output in the correct sequence. There are no repeated blocks or paragraphs.

The heading hierarchy begins at H2 for the title and proceeds correctly to H3 for the 'NOW THEREFORE' section. While an H1 is missing from the document body, the metadata title provides the necessary context for screen readers, and there are no level skips (e.g., H2 to H4).

The signature block at the bottom of the document is represented as a table (reading_order 12). This table uses fabricated headers ('Field' and 'Value') that do not exist in the source. Furthermore, it collapses the side-by-side (2-column) layout of the original signatures into a single column (names/titles are both in the first column), which is a corruption of the document's logical structural organization for these elements.
checks.b_evidence_source: Visual side-by-side layout of signatures for Taylor McCurry (left) and Steve Pitman (right).
checks.b_evidence_output: Table element at index 12 with rows containing names in one column and empty strings in the second, plus fabricated 'Field'/'Value' headers.

checks.a_analysis: The Mitchell County seal image, which is positioned at the top center of the source document header, has been moved to the end of the document in the reading order (element 13, following the text body and signature table). This is a material change to the logical flow of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The Mitchell County seal is located at the top center, between 'NORTH CAROLINA' and 'MITCHELL COUNTY'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The image of the seal has a reading_order of 13, appearing after the entire text body and the signature table.

checks.a_analysis: The document contains a series of 'WHEREAS' clauses which are correctly treated as individual paragraphs, preserving the meaning and layout of the original text. No explicit lists are present.
There are no links in the source document, and none have been incorrectly injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The layout is significantly reorganized compared to the source PDF. In the source, the document follows a logical flow: Header (County Seal and Names) -> Resolution Title -> Whereas Clauses -> Resolved Clause -> Signatures at the bottom. The HTML output fragments this. It first presents the text, then a signature table with missing values, then separate signature images, and finally a high-contrast section that rearranges the content entirely (placing names/titles above the resolution title and body text). This major reorganization breaks the document's logical and visual flow.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF shows a clear top-to-bottom layout: Seal/Header, Title, Body text, and Signatures at the very bottom.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows signatures at the top of the black high-contrast section (e.g., 'Taylor Mc rry' and 'Steve Pitman' appear before 'RESOLUTION SUPPORTING...') and separate images of signatures detached from their labels in the plain-text section.
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the HTML rendering, particularly in the bottom high-contrast section.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML introduces a 'Signatures' table that was not in the original PDF. While the 'Value' column is empty (as signatures are rendered as separate images below), the table itself is rendered with clear borders and legible headers/row text. It is not visually corrupted or overlapping.
The meaningful images from the source—the Mitchell County seal and both handwritten signatures—are present in the HTML output. They appear as standalone images between the text sections.

The entire content of the resolution is duplicated in the HTML output. The full text, including the title, 'WHEREAS' clauses, and the resolution statement, appears once in a plain text/table format and then again in a high-contrast visual block at the bottom. This is a duplication of genuine body content, not just a navigational element.
checks.b_evidence_source: A single instance of the resolution text starting with 'WHEREAS, in September of 2021...'
checks.b_evidence_output: The text 'WHEREAS, in September of 2021...' and all subsequent clauses appear twice on the same page (once in the top white section and once in the bottom black section).
checks.b_evidence_location: HTML output, visible across the entire vertical length of the screenshot.
